coredata.dc:title: Coal derived highly fluorescent N-Doped graphene quantum dots with graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Kundan
Sen, Arindam
Das, Bappaditya
Kumar, Sudip
Chakravarty, Sanchita
Kundu, Rajen
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) constitute a novel category of quantum dots distinguished by their distinctive properties. The introduction of nitrogen heteroatom in GQD is an effective strategy for tuning its intrinsic properties and band gap toward its optoelectronic application. This work explores the synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) derived from low-cost precursor coal. The synthesized N-GQD contains both graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen states along with pyrollic and pyridinic nitrogen states which is the key responsible factor for excellent photoluminescence properties. The excitation dependent photoluminescence of the synthesized N-GQD exhibited excellent emission at 520 nm in neutral and basic pH due to the additional conjugation provided by the doped nitrogen on the surface and functional groups as well. The pH dependent photophysical properties support the existence of doped graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen states which is also well supported by the high resolution N1s XPS peaks at 402 eV (graphitic N) and 406 eV (chemisorbed N/N2). DFT calculations further showed the decrease in fermi energy level by introducing chemisorbed-N. TEM analysis evident the formation of quantum dots with an average diameter of 3 - 7 nm with d-spacing of 0.20 - 0.34 nm. Additionally, the cyclic voltammetry analysis of the synthesized N-GQDs sheds light on the participation of doped nitrogen in redox reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Coal
Chemisorbed nitrogen
N-Doped GQD
pH dependent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD analysis of N-GQD. Raman spectra of (b) N-GQD and DM coal. (c) Magnifying and normalized Raman spectra of N-GQD and DM coal. FT-IR analysis of (d) DM Coal, and (e) N-GQD.
(a) TEM analysis of coal-derived N-GQD. (b) SAED view of N-GQD, (c,d) HRTEM of N-GQD. (e) FFT pattern of HRTEM image of (d). (f) Diameter distribution histogram of (c).
(a) Survey spectrum of N-GQD. High-resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.

(a) UV–visible spectroscopy analysis of aqueous N-GQD at pH 4, 7, and 10. Photoluminescence study of aqueous N-GQD at (b) pH 4 (c) pH 7 (d) pH 10. The inset of (b) represents the emission of aqueous N-GQD at 365 nm.
DFT calculated optimized geometries of the most probable N-GQD structures.
DFT calculated HOMO-LUMO energy levels and band gap of the various N-GQD structures.
CV analysis N-GQD at different scan rates of 10, 15, 25, 50 and 100 mV/s.

head.ce:title: Coal derived highly fluorescent N-Doped graphene quantum dots with graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Applied Chemistry Division, CSIR-National Metallurgical Laboratory, Jamshedpur-831007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aAnalytical and Applied Chemistry Division, CSIR-National Metallurgical Laboratory, Jamshedpur-831007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad-201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bAcademy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad-201002, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) constitute a novel category of quantum dots distinguished by their distinctive properties. The introduction of nitrogen heteroatom in GQD is an effective strategy for tuning its intrinsic properties and band gap toward its optoelectronic application. This work explores the synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) derived from low-cost precursor coal. The synthesized N-GQD contains both graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen states along with pyrollic and pyridinic nitrogen states which is the key responsible factor for excellent photoluminescence properties. The excitation dependent photoluminescence of the synthesized N-GQD exhibited excellent emission at 520 nm in neutral and basic pH due to the additional conjugation provided by the doped nitrogen on the surface and functional groups as well. The pH dependent photophysical properties support the existence of doped graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen states which is also well supported by the high resolution N1s XPS peaks at 402 eV (graphitic N) and 406 eV (chemisorbed N/N2). DFT calculations further showed the decrease in fermi energy level by introducing chemisorbed-N. TEM analysis evident the formation of quantum dots with an average diameter of 3 - 7 nm with d-spacing of 0.20 - 0.34 nm. Additionally, the cyclic voltammetry analysis of the synthesized N-GQDs sheds light on the participation of doped nitrogen in redox reactions.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the investigation for novel nanomaterials has intensified across various scientific disciplines due to their remarkable properties and potential applications in various areas [1]. Among these, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a rapidly developing family of carbon-based nanomaterials due to their exceptional electronic, optical, and chemical characteristics [2]. Their unique features have ignited significant interest in both academic and industrial circles, ranging applications from bioimaging and drug delivery to energy storage and catalysis [3–6]. GQDs, typically sized between 2 and 20 nanometers, are characterized by a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice structure composed of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, giving them more accessible edges, a greater specific surface area, and more surface active sites than graphene [7,8]. Because of these unique properties, GQD exhibits remarkable optical and electrical characteristics, including tunable bandgaps and excellent photoluminescence [9].

Heteroatom doping methodologies are emerging and the most promising strategy to enhance the overall properties of GQDs to overcome their inherent limitations [10,11]. By introducing heteroatoms such as boron, nitrogen, and sulfur into the GQDs lattice, researchers have effectively tuned the bandgap, improved the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), and also achieved better control over the size and size distribution of GQDs [12–14]. The tailored electronic structure offered by heteroatom doping enables it easier to generate localized states within the bandgap, thus, expanding the variety of possible uses for GQDs. Furthermore, the increased photoluminescence quantum yield and optimized size distribution allow for brighter and more effective luminescence, which makes doped GQDs extremely intriguing for optical applications like sensing and bioimaging [15,16]. Moreover, multiple heteroatom doping in graphene quantum dots offers additional advantages over single heteroatom doping, notably through enhanced functionalities and synergistic effects resulting from the coexistence of several heteroatoms. The incorporation of multiple elements such as phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) into GQDs offers a wide range of energy levels within the bandgap, as a result facilitating more effective charge transfer processes and diminished electron-hole recombination rates [17–20]. This phenomenon helps to enhance the quantum yield and photoluminescence properties of the GQDs which makes them better suited for use in photocatalysis and optoelectronics applications [21]. The synergistic interactions between different dopant heteroatoms also encourage the formation of active sites for diverse catalytic reactions and hence enhance the overall efficiency of GQDs in energy conversion and storage devices [22]. Additionally, the combined presence of several heteroatoms allows for fine-tuning of optical properties of the GQDs, including tunable emission wavelengths and enhanced photoresponse characteristics [23]. Overall, the complex interactions between dopant heteroatoms and their combined effects on the structure and functionality of GQDs make multiple element doping an attractive strategy for improving their properties and broadening their application in a variety of disciplines.

The incorporation of different heteroatoms into GQDs results in the influence of different properties of GQD [24–27]. Such as, the incorporation of a nitrogen atom promotes building up additional energy levels within the bandgap to boost the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and diminish the energy loss during recombination [28,29]. While, sulfur doping in GQDs boosts the chemical stability and increases their electrocatalytic activity, making them potential candidates for several applications such as energy storage devices, sensors, and photocatalysis [30,31]. The trend of heteroatom doping in GQDs reveals its widespread and enhanced applications. Yan et al. investigate a 0D/2D van der Waals heterojunctions between 0D graphene quantum dots and 2D graphene sheets, which were prepared from graphite powder [32]. Yang et al. also demonstrated a relationship in intrinsic properties of different heteroatoms (N, P, and S) in bi-functional catalysts in ORR/OER. Their observations reveal that the N atom is the best dopant for ORR/OER [33]. Nair et al. presented N and S co-doped GQD and showed its application as a sensor [34]. Co-doping allows for the optimization of GQDs' optical and electrical properties by adjusting their bandgap, surface chemistry, and electronic structure. Therefore, N doping results in the superior performance of GQDs in terms of chemical stability, photoluminescence quantum yield, and catalytic activity [35,36]. As a result, N-doped GQDs are highly demanding for a variety of technological applications such as biomedicine, optoelectronics, and environmental remediation. However, the multi-step and high-cost synthesis approach associated with GQDs stands as a pivotal challenge that needs immediate resolution [37]. Nitrogen exhibits in four forms in GQD structure, substitutional N atom, interstitial N atom, edge N atom, and adsorbed N atom [38,39]. The most common graphitic N is a substitutional N atom doping, while pyridinic and pyrollic N are edge N atom doping. Also, nitrogen adsorbed on the surface of the GQD structure called adsorbed N atom. All these doped nitrogen influences the intrinsic properties of GQD differently. Such as graphitic N atoms with lone pair electrons provide additional electron density and/or π-electrons in the graphitic network of GQD and thus impart high surface energy, and tunable polarization compared to the non-doped GQD [40]. Moreover, the GQD containing adsorbed nitrogen influences the surface and chemical properties and provides an active site for chemical reactions thus, making GQD a novel material for sensing application and catalysis. Additionally, this adsorbed nitrogen tends to influence the interaction of other molecules with GQD and alter its performance for various applications [41].

Delving into the exploration of simpler synthesis methods for doped GQD, utilizing widely available raw materials for scalable synthesis routes represents a notable gap in the existing literature. The complexities in the current synthesis approach resist widespread adoption and practical applications of GQD on a large scale thus, hindering their potential impact across various fields [42]. To diminish this issue, we present a simple synthesis approach for the preparation of N-GQD using widely available semi-bituminous coal as a precursor. The as-synthesized N-GQD is thoroughly characterized using various analytical techniques and studied its structural, optical, and electrochemical properties. The characterization suggests the presence of graphitic, pyridinic, pyrollic, and adsorbed nitrogen. We investigated the influence of acidic, neutral, and basic pH on the photoluminescence behaviors of the synthesized N-GQD. Overall, the synthesis and characterization of N-doped GQDs from highly abundant and low-cost precursor hold great promise for the development of advanced nanomaterials with tailored properties for a broad range of applications. Thus, this present work contributes to the fundamental understanding of heteroatom-doped GQDs and paves the way for the utilization of abundant and low-cost precursors in the development of functional nanomaterials.

Semi-bituminous coal was collected from the coal mines of the Eastern region of India. Nitric acid (ACS reagent, 70 %), NH4OH (25 %), and Ethanol (99.9 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid and hydrofluoric acid were purchased from SRL Chemicals, India.

The semi-bituminous coal was first demineralized using the previously reported method [43]. In a typical process, semi-bituminous coal was first treated with HCl solution at 90 °C. The solution was filtered and washed with hot deionized water and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C. However, to remove the silica matter, the HCl-treated dried sample was further reacted with HF at 200 °C in a Teflon beaker inside a fuming hood. Then the obtained sample was washed and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C and named DM-Coal.

N-GQD was synthesized via a top-down approach by utilizing semi-bituminous coal. About 1 gm of demineralized coal sample was taken in a conical flask, and mixed with 100 ml 5 M nitric acid. Then, the flask was put into the ultrasonification bath at around 80 °C for 24 h and then heated at 120 °C for another 15 h. After cooling the solution, it was left to settle. After 2 h, the solution was decanted to leave behind the solid/colloidal coal part and the supernatant was collected. For further purification, the supernatant was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and then washed with water, and centrifuged again to collect the supernatant. Finally, the supernatant solution was transferred into a beaker and semi-dried under a vacuum oven at 80 °C to form a jelly paste. To neutralize the solution few dropwise NH4OH was added to it. Again heated under a vacuum oven at 80 °C to evaporate the excess ammonia. The left-behind pasty mass was then mixed with 5 ml of ethanol and kept under a vacuum oven at 80 °C for drying.

The morphological characterization of synthesized N-GQD material was done through scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS-Evo 18). The phase of synthesized N-GQD was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a powder diffractometer (Rigaku Ultima IV) by Cu Kα radiation at 40KV and 40 mA condition with a scan rate of 1° per minute. The defect due to the introduction of nitrogen atoms was examined by Raman spectroscopy (WITEc alpha instrument with a 532 nm laser). The synthesized N-GQD was further investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) by K-Alpha Thermo Fisher Scientific Instruments. The vacuum of the instrument was maintained at 2 × 10−9 mbar and 1.2 × 10−8 mbar in the analyzer chamber and sample loading chamber respectively. XPS data acquisition was done by utilizing monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source operated at 12 kV and 6 mA beam current. The diameter of the X-ray spot size on the sample was 400 μm. Survey scan and high-resolution scan were obtained using pass energies of 200 eV and 50 eV, with energy steps of 1 eV and 0.1 eV respectively. Functional group detection was done by FT-IR spectroscopy in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 and KBr was used to fabricate the sample palate.

The optical property of as-synthesized N-GQD was examined by UV spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU) at pH 4, pH 7, and pH 10 using deionized water as solvent. Similarly, the photoluminescence property of synthesized N-GQD was investigated by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) at different pH levels (pH 4, pH 7, and pH 10). Excitation dependent photoluminescence was measured by exciting the N-GQD sample solution in pH 4, pH 7, and pH 10 at different excitation wavelengths (λex 320 – 480 nm).

The electrochemical performance of prepared N-GQD was investigated by a three-electrode system of Cycling Voltammetry (600+ GAMRY INSTRUMENTS). Graphite was used as a counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl was employed as a reference electrode. The CV analysis was carried out in the potential window of −1.5 V to 1.0 V at different scan rates of 10, 15, 25, 50, and 100 mV/s.

The successful formation of N-doped graphene quantum dot was proved by X-ray Diffraction analysis (Fig. 1 a). The result showed characteristics broad peak at around 2-theta 18° to 27° which matched with the characteristic peak of graphene nanosheet of (022) hkl plane having d-spacing 3.374 Ao. Moreover, the obtained XRD spectrum is well corroborated with the previously reported N-doped GQD [11,32,44,45].

The formation and introduction of nitrogen atoms in GQD were further investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy analysis (Fig. 1d and 1e). The broad peak at around 3200 cm−1 shows the presence of OH and the peaks between 1600 and 1700 cm−1 reveal the presence of the carbonyl group. A small peak of NH overlapped with the OH peak at around 3000 cm−1 and a peak of CN in the fingerprint region at around 1300 cm−1 proves the presence of doped nitrogen in the synthesized N-GQD. The peaks at around 1627 and 1387 cm−1 attributed to aromatic C = C bond vibration and C
H bending vibration respectively (Fig. 1e) [47]. The FT-IR spectrum of DM coal showed a very weak peak at around 1600 cm−1 attributed to aromatic C = C stretching (Fig. 1d) [43]. Moreover, the DM coal did not exhibit significant IR absorption at around 3200 cm−1 corresponding to OH and NH.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) study of N-GQD reveals the transformation of coal into a distinct layered structured compound. The synthesized N-GQD exhibits flake-type morphology which can be seen in Fig. 2 .

Transmission Electron Microscopy was carried out to investigate the structural attributes of N-doped GQD (Fig. 3 ). The TEM images show well-defined GQDs that exhibited a uniform morphology with a diameter in the range of 3–7 nanometers (Fig. 3f), suggesting a controlled synthesis. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Fig. 3b) and FTT pattern (Fig. 3e) of Fig. 3d unveiled the crystalline nature of the synthesized N-GQDs. Specifically, the observed d-spacing values of 0.20 nm, 0.30 nm, and 0.34 nm corresponded to the different lattice planes of GQDs [8,45]. Moreover, the observed alterations in the interlayer spacing and lattice parameters were induced by the dopant nitrogen atoms.

The XPS analysis reveals a comprehensive insight into the chemical composition of synthesized N-GQD. The XPS survey spectrum showed prominent peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s confirming the successful doping of N in GQD as shown in Fig. 4 a. The high-resolution C1s spectrum exhibited distinct peaks at 284.2, 285.8, 286.7, 287.9, and 289.6 eV corresponding to C = C, CC, CN, C = O, and COOH respectively (Fig. 4b) [48,49]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum reveals characteristic nitrogen peaks such as pyridinic N (∼398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (∼400.3 eV), and graphitic N (∼402 eV), along with an additional peak at ∼406 eV refer to the chemisorbed nitrogen which is chemically bonded to GQD (Fig. 4c) [50]. Furthermore, the high-resolution O1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at approximately 533 eV, 532 eV, and 530 eV corresponding to C = O, CO, and -OH respectively. The comprehensive analysis of these XPS peaks provides valuable insights into the chemical structure and composition of N-GQD.

UV visible spectroscopy analysis of the N-GQD was carried out at pH 4, pH 7, and pH 10 (Fig. 5 a). We observed absorbance between 270 and 320 nm with absorbance maxima (λ max) at around 300 nm due to π–π* of C = C of aromatic rings and the absorbance at around 350 nm is due to n–π* of C = N and C = O [47]. The clear higher absorbance of N-GQD in acidic media (at pH 4) than absorbance at neutral and basic media (at pH 10) may be due to the protonation of the surface functional group. However, the redshift absorbance in the neutral and basic pH medium refers to the best light absorbance tendency of synthesized N-GQD at long wavelength (∼450 nm) in the neutral and basic medium compared to the acid medium.

Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis was performed to investigate the photoluminescence behavior in acidic, neutral, and basic mediums. Excitation dependent PL emission measurement was carried out at different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 480 nm at pH 4, pH 7, and pH 10 (Fig. 5b-d). Emission dependent PL excitation measurement was also carried out by fixing different emission wavelengths from 450 nm to 530 nm at pH 4, pH 7, and pH 10 (Figure S1, Supporting information). The excitation dependent PL emission in acidic medium exhibited the maximum emission intensity at 460 nm while in neutral and basic pH medium, the maximum emission intensity was observed at 505 nm and 520 nm respectively. Further, the emission dependent PL excitation spectra of the synthesized N-GQD at various pH levels showed that the maximum excitation intensity in acidic medium was observed at 380 nm while in neutral and basic medium the maximum emission intensity was exhibited at 440 nm and 450 respectively (Figure S1, Supporting information). The above phenomena suggest that in an acidic medium, the proton (H +) reacted with doped nitrogen and decreased the sp2 character which resulted in a decrease in conjugation of electrons. In contrast, the electron density of doped nitrogen in neutral and basic medium resulting increased electronic conjunction in the N-GQD structure and hence, showed the best photo luminescence property in long excitation wavelength [51].

The fluorescence quantum yield of synthesized N-GQD was calculated using the following equation and was found to be 65.6% in neutral pH at 390 nm.
QY = Q
Y
Q S
A
N − G Q D
A
Q S
×
P
L
Q S
P
L
N − G Q D
×
n
N − G Q D
2
n
Q S
2
× 100 %

Here, QY is the fluorescence quantum yield, A is absorbance, PL is the measured integrated fluorescence emission intensity, and n is the refractive index. Since both Quinine Sulphate and N-GQD were dissolved in water solvent the refractive index term become 1 [52].

The role of nitrogen doping was further investigated by DFT calculations with the most probable N-GQD structures. The optimized geometries of the various N-GQD structures are shown in Fig. 6 and the HOMO-LUMO contour plots with band gap energy are given in the supporting information (Section S2-S3).

The electrochemical performance of N-GQD was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 8 ) analysis within a potential window from −1.5 V to 1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in various scan rates of 10, 15, 25, 50, and 100 mV/s. The semi-rectangular shape of CV is attributed to the semiconductor behavior of synthesized material. While non-obvious redox peaks suggest that, the synthesized N-GQD exhibits electrical double-layer capacitor behavior which is better than the faradic capacitor. However, a cathodic peak at around −0.31 V was observed which corresponds to the participation of doped nitrogen in the redox reaction [53].

Semi-bituminous coal was utilized as a precursor to synthesize highly fluorescent N-doped graphene quantum dots. Moreover, the study paved the path to synthesized N-GQD, which contains pyrrolic-N, pyridinic-N, and most importantly graphitic and chemisorbed-N/N2. The as-synthesized N-GQD showed highly pH-dependent optical properties. A blue light absorbance was observed at short wavelength (λabs ≈ 300 nm) due to π–π* transition of C = C of aromatic rings and the absorbance at around 350 nm is due to n–π* transition of C = N and C = O bonds. UV-visible analysis and excitation-dependent photoluminescence analysis of N-GQD in acidic, basic, and neutral pH mediums revealed that the optical properties of N-GQD are highly dependent on the graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen states. Moreover, the high resolution N1s XPS study reveals that synthesized N-GQD contains higher amount of sp3 characterized graphitic, chemisorbed, and pyrrolic nitrogen states. DFT calculations support that the presence of graphitic and chemisorbed nitrogen states significantly decreases the fermi energy level of the N-GQD. The CV analysis also evident the participation of doped nitrogen in redox reactions. Thus, the chemisorbed-N and graphitic-N along with pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N doped GQDs could be potential for applications in both sensors and energy storage materials.

Kundan Kumar: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Arindam Sen: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bappaditya Das: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sudip Kumar: Formal analysis. Sanchita Chakravarty: Writing – review & editing. Rajen Kundu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to CSIR-National Metallurgical Laboratory for research support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2024.113015.
